---
title: "Titanic Survival Analysis"
subtitle: "CIS 435 – Data Analytics Final Report"
author: 
  - "Radu Demian" 
  - "Dmitriy Zhemkov" 
  - "Jacob Foote"
  - "Lui Fuata"
  - "Greg Lampert"
  - "David Reese"
date: 05/05/2026
format: docx
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

# Introduction

The sinking of the RMS Titanic in April 1912 remains one of the most
well-documented maritime disasters in history. Of the 2,224 passengers
and crew aboard, more than 1,500 perished. The tragedy revealed stark
disparities in survival outcomes tied to factors such as passenger
class, sex, age, and fare.

This project was completed by a six-member team consisting of Radu
Demian, Jacob Foote, Lui Fuata, Greg Lampert, David Reese, and Dmitriy
Zhemkov (Team Leader). Collaboration was managed through GitHub and
Discord, where the project repository and Quarto document were
initialized.

The dataset selected is the Titanic passenger dataset sourced from
Kaggle, containing records for 891 passengers across 12 variables
including demographics, socioeconomic status, family composition,
embarkation port, and survival status.

## Objectives

The specific objectives of this project are:

- Understand the structure and characteristics of the Titanic dataset
- Clean and prepare the data for analysis
- Explore patterns and relationships through visualizations and
  statistical summaries
- Engineer new features to improve predictive power
- Build and evaluate multiple classification models
- Compare model performance and identify the best predictor of survival
- Translate findings into meaningful real-world insights

### Problem Statement

The central question driving this analysis is: What factors most
influenced whether a passenger survived the Titanic disaster?

Specifically, this analysis addresses the following questions:

- How did passenger class affect survival?
- Did traveling with family improve survival compared to traveling
  alone?
- Did passengers who paid higher fares have better survival odds?
- Which combination of attributes best predicts survival?
- Can we build a model that predicts survival with meaningful accuracy?
  \# Dataset Overview

The dataset used in this analysis is the Titanic passenger dataset
obtained from Kaggle. It contains 891 rows and 12 columns, where each
row represents a single passenger.

## Variables

The following variables are included in the dataset:

| Variable | Type | Description |
|------------------------|------------------|------------------------------|
| PassengerId | Numeric | Unique identifier for each passenger |
| Survived | Numeric | Survival status (0 = Died, 1 = Survived) |
| Pclass | Numeric | Passenger class (1 = First, 2 = Second, 3 = Third) |
| Name | Character | Full name of the passenger |
| Sex | Character | Sex of the passenger |
| Age | Numeric | Age in years |
| SibSp | Numeric | Number of siblings/spouses aboard |
| Parch | Numeric | Number of parents/children aboard |
| Ticket | Character | Ticket number |
| Fare | Numeric | Passenger fare paid |
| Cabin | Character | Cabin number |
| Embarked | Character | Port of embarkation (C = Cherbourg, Q = Queenstown, S = Southampton) |

## Data Quality

Initial inspection revealed the following data quality issues that were
addressed during cleaning:

- **Age** contained 177 missing values and was imputed using the median
- **Cabin** contained 687 missing values — the deck letter was extracted
  where available and the rest labeled as Unknown
- **Embarked** contained 2 missing values
- No duplicate records were found in the dataset

# Methodology

## Week 1 — Data Preparation and Cleaning

The dataset contains passenger information from the RMS Titanic sinking,
including variables such as survival status, passenger class, sex, age,
and fare. There are several missing values in the age, cabin, and
embarked columns, but no duplicate records were found.

We will use the data to analyze the factors that affected survival
status among the passengers.

survived (0 = No, 1 = Yes)

Key Predictors: pclass, sex, age, sib_sp, parch, fare, embarked

There are a lot of missing values in this dataset. No duplicates.

The summary statistics show that age is spread across a wide range,
indicating a diverse group of passengers. Fare is highly skewed, with
most passengers paying lower amounts and a small number paying very high
prices. The survival variable is imbalanced, more passengers died than
survived. Because of the skewness in fare, it may need to be adjusted or
capped for better analysis.

The age distribution appears slightly skewed. Fare is heavily
right-skewed, confirming the presence of outliers. Most passengers
traveled with few or no family members, as shown by the sib_sp and parch
distributions. The survival count shows that more passengers died than
survived. The majority of passengers were in 3rd class, and there were
more males than females on board. Most passengers embarked from
Southampton. These distributions suggest that class, sex, and fare may
be important factors in survival.

The boxplots show that passengers who survived generally paid higher
fares compared to those who did not survive. The fare variable contains
extreme outliers, which can distort analysis.

After capping fare at the 99th percentile, the distribution becomes more
stable while still keeping most of the data, making further analysis
more reliable.

## Weeks 2–3 — Exploratory Data Analysis and Feature Engineering

The univariate analysis shows how each variable is distributed on its
own. Age is somewhat normally distributed but slightly skewed,
indicating a mix of younger and older passengers. Fare is heavily
right-skewed, with most passengers paying low amounts and a few paying
very high prices, which confirms the presence of outliers. The survival
distribution shows that more passengers died than survived, so the
outcome is imbalanced. Passenger class distribution shows that most
people were in 3rd class, while fewer were in 1st and 2nd class. The sex
distribution shows that there were more males than females on board.

The correlation analysis shows a negative relationship between passenger
class and fare, meaning higher-class passengers paid more. There is also
a negative relationship between passenger class and survival, indicating
that higher-class passengers were more likely to survive. Most other
relationships are weak, suggesting that survival is influenced by
multiple variables rather than a single strong factor.

The scatter plots show that passengers who paid higher fares were more
likely to survive. Lower-class passengers had lower survival rates,
reinforcing the importance of socioeconomic status. Age does not show a
strong or clear relationship with survival, other than the fact that if
you were in a lower class you were more likely to survive if you were
younger. Overall, fare and class appear to be more important predictors
than age.

The group comparisons show clear differences in survival rates. Females
had a much higher survival rate than males, and passengers in higher
classes were more likely to survive than those in lower classes. Fare
also varies significantly by class, with higher-class passengers paying
more. These patterns suggest that both gender and socioeconomic status
played a major role in determining survival.

Several new variables were created to better capture passenger
characteristics. FamilySize represents the total number of family
members aboard, while IsAlone indicates whether a passenger was
traveling alone. Deck was extracted from the cabin information, even
though a lot of values were missing and labeled as unknown. GroupSize
shows how many passengers shared the same ticket, and FareBin
categorizes fares into levels. The cutoffs at 8, 15, and 31 roughly
separate low-cost tickets from mid-range, higher, and very expensive
fares based on how the data is distributed. Since fare is heavily
right-skewed, most values are concentrated at the lower end, so smaller
intervals are used there, while a larger range captures the high-end
outliers. These new features provide more structure to the data and may
improve future analysis.

These visualizations help confirm that the engineered features and fare
bins capture meaningful patterns in the data. The fare distribution with
cutoffs shows that most values are concentrated in the lower ranges,
which justifies the bin choices. The survival-by-bin plot shows clear
differences across fare levels, supporting the idea that fare is linked
to survival. Similarly, features like FamilySize, IsAlone, Deck, and
GroupSize reveal patterns that are not obvious in the raw data, making
them useful for further analysis.

## Week 4 — Baseline Modeling

Two baseline models were built: a linear regression model to predict
fare, and a logistic regression model to predict survival. The dataset
was split 70% training and 30% test.

## Week 5 — Advanced Modeling

The SVM model used an 80/20 train/test split with 5-fold
cross-validation and achieved 71.83% accuracy. It is better at
predicting non-survivors (Sensitivity = 0.7857) than survivors
(Specificity = 0.627). A Gradient Boosting model was implemented using
XGBoost via the tidymodels framework, achieving 78.97% accuracy. \#
Results and Findings

## Week 1 — Data Preparation

The dataset contains 891 rows and 12 columns. Missing values were found
in three columns: Age (177 missing), Cabin (687 missing), and Embarked
(2 missing). No duplicate records were found. Fare was capped at the
99th percentile to handle extreme outliers.

## Weeks 2–3 — Exploratory Data Analysis

Univariate analysis confirmed that fare is heavily right-skewed and that
more passengers died than survived. The majority of passengers were in
3rd class, and there were more males than females on board.

Bivariate analysis revealed the following key patterns:

- Females had a significantly higher survival rate than males
- First-class passengers survived at higher rates than second and third
  class
- Passengers who paid higher fares were more likely to survive
- Age showed no strong relationship with survival on its own

## Week 4 — Baseline Model Results

The linear regression model predicting fare produced an RMSE of 42.44,
indicating moderate prediction error given the wide range and skewness
of fare values.

The logistic regression model predicting survival achieved the following
on the test set:

| Metric    | Value  |
|-----------|--------|
| Accuracy  | 80.84% |
| Precision | 79.49% |
| Recall    | 71.26% |

## Week 5 — Advanced Model Results

Three additional models were evaluated on the test set:

| Model         | Accuracy |
|---------------|----------|
| Decision Tree | 83.64%   |
| Random Forest | 81.78%   |
| SVM           | 71.83%   |
| XGBoost       | 78.97%   |

## Week 6 — Final Model Comparison

All five models were compared by accuracy on the test set. The final
rankings were:

| Rank | Model               | Accuracy |
|------|---------------------|----------|
| 1    | Decision Tree       | 83.64%   |
| 2    | Random Forest       | 81.78%   |
| 3    | Logistic Regression | 80.84%   |
| 4    | XGBoost             | 78.97%   |
| 5    | SVM                 | 71.83%   |

The Decision Tree achieved the highest accuracy at 83.64%, with Random
Forest close behind at 81.78%. \# Conclusions and Business Insights \#
Conclusions and Business Insights

## Key Findings

The analysis identified several factors that strongly influenced
survival on the Titanic:

- **Sex** was the strongest predictor of survival. Females survived at a
  much higher rate than males, consistent with the "women and children
  first" evacuation policy.
- **Passenger class** had a significant impact. First-class passengers
  were more likely to survive than second and third class passengers,
  reflecting the role of socioeconomic status.
- **Fare** was positively associated with survival. Passengers who paid
  higher fares, largely those in first class, had better survival odds.
- **Age** did not show a strong individual relationship with survival,
  though younger passengers in lower classes had slightly better odds.
- **Traveling alone** was associated with lower survival rates compared
  to traveling with family.

## Model Performance

The Decision Tree achieved the highest test accuracy at 83.64%, followed
closely by Random Forest at 81.78%. While the Decision Tree performed
best on this dataset, Random Forest is generally considered more robust
due to its resistance to overfitting. Logistic Regression performed
competitively at 80.84% while remaining the most interpretable model.
SVM performed the weakest at 71.83%.

## Business Insights

These findings have real-world implications beyond historical analysis:

- Socioeconomic status and resource access play a critical role in
  emergency outcomes. This pattern is observable in modern disaster
  response scenarios where evacuation prioritization and resource
  allocation can determine survival.
- Predictive modeling can be applied to emergency planning to identify
  at-risk populations and improve evacuation protocols.
- The strong performance of tree-based models suggests that survival
  outcomes follow clear decision rules based on a small number of key
  variables, particularly sex, class, and fare.

## Limitations

- The dataset contains 177 missing age values, which were removed during
  cleaning and may have introduced bias.
- The cabin variable had 687 missing values, limiting the usefulness of
  the Deck feature.
- Models were trained on a relatively small dataset of 891 passengers,
  which may limit generalizability.
